title: 8 Herbal Decoctions Proven to Help with Premenstrual Syndrome

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Polyphenols are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-anxiety and antispasmodic properties that help reduce stress and ease muscle cramps associated with PMS.

Paeoniflorin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress, promotes relaxation, and improves sleep quality, which are all common symptoms that people experience during PMS.
Flavonoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-anxiety and anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness.
Tannins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-anxiety properties and flavonoids that help reduce symptoms of stress, anxiety, and mood swings associated with PMS.

Quercetin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce inflammation and calm the nervous system, which can ease symptoms such as mood swings, anxiety, and bloating associated with PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Paeonia lactiflora decoction is a traditional herbal remedy that may help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). It contains a compound called paeoniflorin, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce bloating and cramps. The decoction may also help regulate hormonal imbalances by influencing the production of estrogen and progesterone, two key hormones involved in the menstrual cycle. This can lead to a reduction in mood swings and irritability, common symptoms of PMS. Additionally, Paeonia lactiflora has been shown to have a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help alleviate anxiety and stress associated with PMS. By reducing inflammation, regulating hormones, and promoting relaxation, Paeonia lactiflora decoction may provide relief from the physical and emotional symptoms of PMS.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Passiflora incarnata herbal decoction that can help with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering dried Passiflora incarnata leaves, flowers, and stems. Use one teaspoon of the dried herbs for every cup of boiling water. Place the herbs in a saucepan and add the boiling water, then reduce the heat to a simmer and let it steep for 5-10 minutes. You can also add a sweetener like honey or sugar to make it more palatable. Once the decoction is ready, strain it into a cup and discard the solids. Let the decoction cool down before consuming it, ideally 1-2 cups a day, 1-2 days before the expected menstrual period. It's essential to note that Passiflora incarnata can interact with certain medications, so consult with a healthcare professional before using it, especially if you're already taking any prescription medications.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerols are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce stress, anxiety, and mood swings associated with PMS.

Shogaols are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to reduce anxiety and stress by acting on the brain's GABA receptors, which can help to regulate and calm the nervous system, leading to relief from symptoms of PMS.
Gingerol may be good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce anxiety and stress associated with PMS.

Shogaol is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-anxiety properties that help reduce stress and calm the nervous system, making it easier to manage symptoms such as mood swings and irritability.
Zingiberene is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce symptoms of anxiety and mood swings often experienced during this time.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, has been used for centuries to help with symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). A decoction, or a strong tea, made from ginger root can be a natural remedy for PMS. Ginger contains compounds that help to reduce inflammation and pain, which can be a major problem for women during their menstrual cycle. Ginger decoctions have been shown to ease cramps, bloating, and mood swings that are common during PMS. The anti-inflammatory properties of ginger can also help to reduce water retention and breast tenderness, making it a popular natural remedy for PMS symptoms. Additionally, ginger has been found to have a calming effect on the body, which can help to reduce stress and anxiety that women often experience during PMS. Many women find that drinking a ginger decoction during their menstrual cycle helps to alleviate their symptoms and improves their overall well-being.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Passiflora incarnata herbal decoction that helps with premenstrual syndrome (PMS), start by gathering dried or fresh Passiflora incarnata flowers. If using dried flowers, soak 1-2 teaspoons in a cup of boiling water for 5-7 minutes. If using fresh flowers, use 3-4 teaspoons and soak for 10-15 minutes. Strain the mixture using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve, and discard the solids. Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired. Let the decoction cool down, then refrigerate it. Take 1/2 cup of the cooled decoction 2-3 times a day, 2-3 days before your period starts, and continue until your symptoms subside. You can also mix it with other herbal teas, like chamomile or peppermint, for a soothing and calming effect. However, consult with a healthcare professional or a qualified herbalist before using Passiflora incarnata, especially if you're pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking medications.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Ligustilide is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has been shown to reduce anxiety and depression by acting as a GABA receptor agonist and inhibiting the activity of inflammatory cytokines, which contribute to the symptoms of PMS.
Butylphthalide is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-anxiety and anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce symptoms of stress, mood swings, and bloating associated with PMS.

Flavonoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-anxiety and anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce symptoms such as stress, mood swings, and physical discomfort associated with PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Angelica sinensis decoctions, also known as Dong Quai, is a traditional Chinese medicine used to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). PMS can cause painful cramps, mood swings, and bloating, making life uncomfortable for many women. The active compounds in Angelica sinensis, such as ferulic acid and ligustilide, have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce menstrual cramps and discomfort. These compounds also have a soothing effect on the uterus, which can help calm down spasms and reduce pain. Additionally, Angelica sinensis contains antioxidants that can help regulate hormonal imbalances, which are often a contributing factor to PMS symptoms. The decoction has been shown to improve blood circulation, which can help reduce bloating and water retention. Many women have reported relief from PMS symptoms after using Angelica sinensis decoctions, and it is often recommended by practitioners of traditional Chinese medicine as a natural remedy for PMS.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Passiflora incarnata herbal decoction that helps with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering fresh or dried Passionflower leaves and flowers. You will need about 1 teaspoon of dried herbs or 2 teaspoons of fresh herbs for every cup of boiling water. Combine the herbs with 1 cup of boiling water in a saucepan and let it steep for 5-10 minutes. If using fresh herbs, you may want to strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve to remove any solids. For dried herbs, simply let it steep and then strain the mixture. Once the decoction has cooled, you can store it in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours. To use, drink 1/2 to 1 cup of the Passiflora incarnata decoction 2-3 times a day, preferably 1-2 weeks before your period starts. You can also add honey or lemon to taste. It's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal remedy, especially if you're pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking medications.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Phenolic acids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce stress and anxiety, and their flavonoids also act as a natural sedative to promote relaxation and alleviate symptoms.

Actein is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress by acting as a sedative and anxiolytic, which calms the nervous system and alleviates symptoms such as irritability, mood swings, and restlessness associated with PMS.
Isoferulic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-anxiety and anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce symptoms like mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness.
Flavonoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have a calming effect on the body, reducing anxiety and stress that often accompany PMS symptoms.
Tannins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce anxiety, stress, and mood swings associated with PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cimicifuga racemosa decoctions have been used for many years to help women with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). This herb is also known as black cohosh. It's believed to help with PMS symptoms like mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness. Cimicifuga racemosa is thought to have a balancing effect on hormones in the body, which can help to reduce the severity of PMS symptoms. It may also have a calming effect on the nervous system, which can help to reduce anxiety and irritability that often accompany PMS. Some studies suggest that Cimicifuga racemosa decoctions may be as effective as prescription medications in reducing PMS symptoms. However, more research is needed to confirm this. The active compounds in Cimicifuga racemosa, including triterpene glycosides and phenolic acids, are thought to be responsible for its therapeutic effects. These compounds may help to reduce inflammation and improve hormone balance in the body.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Passiflora incarnata herbal decoction that helps with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering fresh or dried Passiflora incarnata flowers. You can purchase dried flowers from a local health food store or grow your own plant. If using fresh flowers, clean them thoroughly and dry them in a warm place or using a food dehydrator. For a decoction, combine one to two teaspoons of dried flowers or one tablespoon of fresh flowers with 1 cup of boiling water in a saucepan. Allow the mixture to simmer for 5-10 minutes, then strain the liquid using a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a cup. Discard the solids and let the decoction cool. Once cooled, you can drink the decoction as a tea, ideally 2-3 times a day, starting a week before your period is expected to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as anxiety and mood swings. It's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal remedies, especially if you're taking medications or have underlying health conditions.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Valerenic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress, promote relaxation, and improve sleep quality, which are often disrupted symptoms associated with PMS.
Valeranone is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has a calming effect on the body, which helps to reduce symptoms of anxiety, stress, and mood swings often experienced during this time.

Valerenal is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress by acting on the brain's GABA receptors, which can help to regulate mood swings and other symptoms associated with PMS.

Isovaleric acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce symptoms of anxiety and stress, which are often associated with PMS, by acting as a natural sedative and promoting relaxation in the body.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis, also known as valerian root, is a popular herbal remedy used to ease symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The decoction, a liquid extract made by steeping the root in hot water, is believed to have a calming effect on the body. The active compounds in valerian root, such as isovaleric acid and valerenic acid, have been shown to interact with the brain's neurotransmitters, reducing anxiety and stress. This can help alleviate symptoms like mood swings, irritability, and restlessness that are common during PMS. Additionally, valerian root has been found to have a sedative effect, promoting relaxation and improving sleep quality, which is often disrupted during the premenstrual phase. By reducing stress and promoting relaxation, valerian root decoction may help women feel more comfortable and in control during PMS.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Passiflora incarnata herbal decoction that can help with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering dried and organic passionflower leaves and flowers. You will need one teaspoon of the dried herb for every cup of boiling water. Next, boil water in a pot and add the teaspoon of dried Passiflora incarnata to it. Let the mixture simmer for about 5 to 10 minutes, allowing the herb to infuse into the water. Then, remove the pot from the heat source and let it sit for a few minutes to allow the decoction to cool down slightly. Strain the mixture using a fine-mesh sieve or a piece of cheesecloth into a cup, pressing on the solids to extract as much liquid as possible. Discard the solids and drink the decoction, which can be taken 2-3 times a day as needed to help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms such as anxiety, mood swings, and insomnia.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Bilobalide is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has a calming effect on the mind and body, reducing anxiety and stress that often come with PMS.

Quercetin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce anxiety and mood swings associated with PMS, and also acts as an antioxidant to calm the nervous system.

Isorhapontigenin helps to alleviate premenstrual syndrome because it acts as a natural anti-anxiety agent by reducing stress hormones and promoting relaxation, which can help to ease symptoms of PMS such as mood swings, anxiety, and irritability.
Flavonoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to reduce anxiety, improve sleep, and balance hormone levels by acting as a natural sedative and having anti-inflammatory properties.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba decoctions may help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. This is because ginkgo biloba has blood vessel-widening properties, which can increase blood flow to the brain and other parts of the body. Improved blood flow may help reduce symptoms like headaches and migraines that are common during PMS. Ginkgo biloba also has anti-inflammatory properties, which may help lower levels of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that can cause cramps, bloating, and breast tenderness during PMS. Additionally, ginkgo biloba may help stabilize mood swings by influencing neurotransmitter levels in the brain. Some research suggests that ginkgo biloba can also have a positive effect on anxiety and depression, which are often linked to PMS. However, more studies are needed to fully understand the benefits of ginkgo biloba for PMS relief.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Passiflora incarnata herbal decoction for premenstrual syndrome (PMS), start by gathering 1-2 teaspoons of dried Passiflora incarnata flowers or leaves. You can purchase these from a health food store or online. Next, add the dried herbs to 1 cup of boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. This allows the herbs to release their active compounds, including flavonoids, alkaloids, and glycosides, which have been shown to have a calming effect on the nervous system and help alleviate PMS symptoms such as anxiety, mood swings, and insomnia. Once the decoction has steeped, strain it through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup, discarding the solids. You can drink the decoction warm or at room temperature, up to 3 times a day, as needed. It's essential to note that while Passiflora incarnata may provide relief from PMS symptoms, it's not a substitute for medical treatment. Always consult with a healthcare professional before using herbal remedies, especially if you're pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking medications.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Linalool is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-anxiety properties and helps reduce stress and tension, which are common symptoms of PMS, allowing women to feel more calm and relaxed.
Linalyl acetate is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress by promoting relaxation and calming the nervous system, which is often disrupted during PMS.

Rosmarinic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help reduce symptoms of anxiety, depression, and mood swings associated with PMS.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia, also known as English lavender, is often used to help with symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). The decoctions, or infusions, made from the flowers are thought to be good for PMS because they contain compounds that can help relax the body and mind. Lavandula angustifolia has anti-anxiety and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help to reduce symptoms of anxiety, irritability, and breast tenderness that often come with PMS. Additionally, the herb is believed to have a balancing effect on hormones, which can help to regulate menstrual cycles and reduce symptoms of mood swings and bloating. The calming effects of Lavandula angustifolia may also help to improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted during PMS. Some people find that taking a warm bath with Lavandula angustifolia or drinking the decoction as a tea can help to soothe their body and mind, and reduce the severity of PMS symptoms.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a herbal decoction from Passiflora incarnata to help with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering 2-3 teaspoons of dried and crushed Passiflora incarnata root or 4-6 teaspoons of fresh root. Place the root in a saucepan and add 2 cups of water, making sure the root is completely covered. Bring the mixture to a boil over medium heat, then reduce the heat to low and let it simmer for 10-15 minutes. Next, turn off the heat and let the decoction steep for 5-10 minutes. Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl, discarding the solids. Let the decoction cool down to room temperature before transferring it to a glass bottle. Store the decoction in the refrigerator and use 1/2 to 1 cup of it as needed, ideally 2-3 times a day, to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as anxiety, irritability, and mood swings.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Flavonoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they help to calm the nervous system, reduce anxiety and stress, and balance hormones, which can lead to a decrease in symptoms such as mood swings, irritability, and breast tenderness.
Polyphenols are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to reduce anxiety, stress, and mood swings associated with PMS.
Tannins are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have a calming effect on the body, which can help reduce anxiety and mood swings that often occur before a woman's period starts.

Quercetin is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps reduce inflammation and anxiety by acting as a natural antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, which in turn can ease symptoms such as mood swings, bloating, and breast tenderness.

Kaempferol is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress by acting as a natural anti-inflammatory and antioxidant, which in turn can help to ease the symptoms of PMS such as mood swings and bloating.

remedies.remedy_desc: Passiflora incarnata, also known as passionflower, is a plant that has been used for centuries to help with anxiety and sleep problems. For women who experience premenstrual syndrome (PMS), passionflower decoctions can be a useful remedy. One reason is that it contains flavonoids and alkaloids, which are natural substances that can help to calm the nervous system and reduce stress. This can be especially helpful during the premenstrual phase, when many women feel anxious and overwhelmed. Passionflower decoctions have also been shown to have a sedative effect, which can help to regulate sleep patterns and improve the quality of sleep. This is important, as many women with PMS struggle with insomnia and other sleep disorders. Additionally, passionflower has been found to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce cramps and other physical symptoms associated with PMS. By reducing anxiety and promoting relaxation, passionflower decoctions may be able to help women manage their PMS symptoms and improve their overall well-being.
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make a Passiflora incarnata herbal decoction that helps with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering 1-2 teaspoons of dried Passiflora incarnata leaves or flowers, depending on the desired strength. Next, boil 1 cup of water in a pot, then add the dried herbs and let them steep for 5-7 minutes. After the steeping time has passed, strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a cup or container. Discard the solids and let the decoction cool down to a comfortable temperature. Once cooled, you can drink the Passiflora incarnata decoction 2-3 times a day, ideally 1 week before the menstrual period starts, to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as anxiety, mood swings, and cramps. However, it is recommended to consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal remedy, especially if you are pregnant, breastfeeding, or taking any medications.

intro_desc: Herbal decoctions can be a helpful remedy for premenstrual syndrome (PMS), a common condition in women where mood swings, bloating, and other symptoms occur before menstruation. One herb that has been shown to be effective in alleviating these symptoms is Paeonia lactiflora, also known as peony root. It has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce bloating and ease cramps. Paeonia lactiflora also has a calming effect on the mind and body, which can help reduce anxiety and mood swings associated with PMS.  Another herb that can help with PMS symptoms is Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger. Ginger has natural anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce pain and bloating. It also has a calming effect on the digestive system, which can help alleviate symptoms of nausea and indigestion that often accompany PMS.  Fixing PMS symptoms is important because they can significantly impact daily life. Women who experience severe PMS symptoms may find it difficult to perform daily tasks, maintain relationships, and maintain their usual level of productivity. PMS symptoms can also affect a woman's mental health, leading to anxiety, depression, and feelings of hopelessness. By addressing PMS symptoms with herbal decoctions like Paeonia lactiflora and Zingiber officinale, women can improve their overall quality of life and reduce the impact of PMS on their daily lives.  To improve life with PMS, women can try incorporating herbal decoctions into their self-care routine. This can include drinking a cup of decoction made from Paeonia lactiflora and Zingiber officinale tea before bedtime to help alleviate symptoms and promote relaxation. Women can also experiment with different herbal combinations to find what works best for them. By taking a proactive approach to managing PMS symptoms, women can regain control over their lives and live a more balanced and healthy lifestyle.
supplementary_best: A combination of 'Angelica sinensis', 'Paeonia lactiflora', and 'Valeriana officinalis' can help remedy premenstrual syndrome. 'Angelica sinensis' is known to ease symptoms of bloating and water retention, which are common in PMS. 'Paeonia lactiflora' has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce cramps and breast tenderness. 'Valeriana officinalis' is a natural sedative that can help with mood swings and anxiety.  To prepare this herbal decoction, combine 2 teaspoons of dried 'Angelica sinensis', 1 teaspoon of dried 'Paeonia lactiflora', and 1 teaspoon of dried 'Valeriana officinalis' in 1 quart of boiling water. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain and drink 1/2 cup, 2-3 times a day. The anti-inflammatory and sedative properties of these herbs will help reduce symptoms of PMS, making it easier to manage.  This combination works because 'Angelica sinensis' and 'Paeonia lactiflora' address the physical symptoms of PMS, while 'Valeriana officinalis' addresses the emotional symptoms. By combining these herbs, you can get relief from a range of PMS symptoms, making it a more effective remedy than using individual herbs.
supplementary_avoid: If you have premenstrual syndrome (PMS), there are some herbal decoctions you should avoid.  One such herb is 'Hydrastis canadensis' or goldenseal, which may interact with birth control pills and make them less effective. This could lead to unintended pregnancy.  Another herb to be cautious with is 'Glycyrrhiza glabra' or licorice root, which can cause high blood pressure and worsen conditions like heart disease.  'Artemisia absinthium' or wormwood is also not recommended, as it can cause stomach problems and worsen symptoms of PMS like bloating and cramps.  'Euonymus europaea' or spindle tree is another herb to avoid, as it can cause dizziness and nausea.  Lastly, 'Taxus baccata' or yew is highly toxic and can cause serious health problems, including death, if ingested.  It's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal decoctions, especially if you have PMS or are taking medication.
supplementary_other_remedies: Dietary changes can help alleviate premenstrual syndrome symptoms. Eating foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids, such as salmon and flaxseeds, can reduce inflammation and ease mood swings. Magnesium-rich foods like dark leafy greens and nuts can help with cramps and anxiety. Some people find relief in probiotic-rich foods like yogurt and kefir, which support gut health and may reduce symptoms of bloating and digestive issues.  In addition to diet, stress management techniques like meditation and deep breathing can help mitigate symptoms of PMS. Aromatherapy with essential oils like lavender and peppermint can also promote relaxation and reduce anxiety. Regular exercise, such as yoga or brisk walking, can help reduce symptoms of bloating, fatigue, and irritability. Getting enough sleep is also crucial, as fatigue is a common symptom of PMS.
supplementary_related_ailments: People with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) often experience other health issues that can be managed with medicinal herbs. Many of these herbs have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help alleviate symptoms. For instance, women with PMS are more likely to experience bloating and water retention, which can be relieved with dandelion root tea, as it helps to reduce fluid retention and balance electrolytes.  Women with PMS are also more prone to anxiety and depression, which can be eased with St. John's Wort, a herb that contains compounds that help regulate mood and reduce stress. Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate menstrual cramps, which are common among women with PMS.  In addition, women with PMS are more likely to experience fatigue, which can be boosted with ashwagandha, an herb that is rich in antioxidants and helps to balance hormones. Another issue that people with PMS often face is insomnia, which can be improved with valerian root tea, a natural sedative that promotes relaxation and helps with sleep.  Red clover is another herb that has been found to be beneficial for women with PMS, as it contains isoflavones that help to balance estrogen levels, which can contribute to PMS symptoms. Black cohosh is also known to help alleviate hot flashes and mood swings associated with PMS. These herbs can be consumed in various forms, such as teas, capsules, or tinctures, under the guidance of a healthcare professional.
